text: Lin was a literate, fascinating woman who lived for photography of the night sky. She refused to be passive about the cosmic wonders above her, and her relentless, perpetual curiosity would kindle in anyone who met her. From a lofty hill in Asia she would traverse rough, jagged trails, carrying a heavy pail of gear and an old cane for balance. She was no senseless dreamer; with impartial, effective method she would determine the depth of every shadow and clamp her camera to a horizontal mount. The exotic substance of starlight, she said, was a fantasy made workable by patient craft. Anyone could guess at the stars, but only the devoted could surmount the discrepancy between hope and a real, appreciable image. Her lover, a quiet priest who had left the church to study democracy, would couch his praise in gentle tone. He would readily provide her a guitar tune while she worked slowly, and the harmonious living room felt full of calm. Once a report reached them: a friend had died, and they sent a card of condolence. Grief, she insisted, must not jettison joy. Her coordination of fume-filled darkroom chemicals, an additive here, a gimmick there, was a quiet exploitation of old methods. She would not pose for any poster; she chose to lurk behind the lens. On the wall hung a faded stripe of film, a phoenix rising, an angel of light, a goddess of the void. The mercury in her thermometer barely moved on cold nights, yet she would not give up. Her intercontinental fame grew, though it felt delusive; one conditional grant would pay her wage, and she would pound the desk over red tape. The FBI once asked about an assassination plot near her vault of negatives, but the rumor proved pointless and false. By a score of careful exposures, every component checked, she made an adaptation of an ancient star chart, layday by layday refining each line. A gallon of developer, a sharpener for her pencils, a sheet of coinage-bright foil thereon, and the rusty shutter that would creak: all of it survived. She would shut the iron shutter against the wind, watch a wave of cloud roll past, and trust that, in any circumstance, the creation above held a comparatively simple certainty. The feeble light of the oblique horizon, the debris of a meteor, the gentle oblique slant of dawn over the region, were enough. A couple who lived nearby would visit her, and she would insist that they choose a clear night to watch with him, her lover, while the boys played baseball in the yard below. She liked to transfuse her own wonder into them, telling of every happening in the sky, of rust on the old dome and the way starlight helps the soul survive. She had survived, and so had her wonder.
translation: 林是一位有文化、迷人的女性，她为拍摄夜空而活。她拒绝对头顶的宇宙奇观漠然处之，她那不屈不挠、永不停歇的好奇心会点燃任何遇见她的人。从亚洲一座高耸的山丘上，她会穿越崎岖锯齿状的小径，提着一桶沉重的器材，拄着一根用来保持平衡的旧手杖。她不是一个愚钝的空想者；她以公正而有效的方法测定每一道阴影的深浅，并把相机夹紧在水平支架上。她说，星光这种奇异的物质，是一种被耐心手艺变得可行的幻想。任何人都能对星辰妄加猜测，但只有虔诚的人才能克服希望与真实可见影像之间的差距。她的爱人是一位安静的牧师，他离开了教会去研究民主，他会用温和的语调含蓄地表达赞美。她慢慢工作时，他会欣然为她弹一段吉他曲，那和谐的客厅充满了宁静。一次有消息传来：一位朋友去世了，他们寄出了一张吊唁卡。她坚持认为，悲伤绝不能抛弃欢乐。她对充满烟雾的暗房化学药品的协调——这里加一种添加剂，那里用一个小花招——是对旧方法的一种悄然利用。她不愿为任何海报摆姿势；她选择潜伏在镜头之后。墙上挂着一段褪色的条状胶片、一只腾起的凤凰、一位光之天使、一位虚空的女神。寒夜里她温度计中的水银几乎不动，但她绝不放弃。她那洲际般的名声不断增长，却显得虚妄；一笔有条件的拨款将支付她的工资，而她会为繁文缛节捶桌。联邦调查局曾就她的底片金库附近的一桩暗杀阴谋来询问，但传言被证明毫无意义且不实。通过一二十次精心曝光，每个部件都经过检查，她对一幅古老的星图做了改编，一个停泊日又一个停泊日地打磨每一条线。一加仑显影液、一支削铅笔的卷笔刀、一张其上铺着钱币般光亮的箔纸，以及那会吱嘎作响的生锈快门：这一切都留存了下来。她会关上铁制百叶窗以抵御风，望着一片云浪滚过，并相信无论在任何情形下，头顶的造物都蕴含着相对简单的确定性。倾斜地平线上微弱的光、一颗流星的残骸、黎明在这片地区上方那温柔的斜照，都已足够。住在附近的一对夫妇会来拜访她，她会坚持要他们挑一个晴朗的夜晚和她的爱人一起观星，而下面院子里男孩们正在打棒球。她喜欢把自己的惊奇之情灌输给他们，讲述天空中每一桩事件、旧穹顶上的锈迹，以及星光如何帮助灵魂存活。她活了下来，她的惊奇之心也是。
